coredata.dc:title: Methyl formate and dimethyl ether electro-oxidation on PtPdSn catalyst supported on carbon nanotube decorated with carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, V.B.
Kashyap, D.
Teller, H.
Gebru, M.G.
Gedanken, A.
Schechter, A.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, methyl formate (MF) and dimethyl ether (DME) electro-oxidation was studied on equimolar ratio PtPdSn catalyst supported on Vulcan Carbon XC-72, carbon nanotubes (CNT), and CNT–nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) composite. The ternary catalyst was synthesized by the ethylene glycol-assisted thermal reduction method and NCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method in the presence of CNT to form CNT-NCDs composite, in which the NCDs are incorporated onto the CNT surface. The activity of the catalyst in the oxidation of MF and DME was analyzed using cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry techniques. The ternary catalyst supported on CNT-NCDs composite (Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs) showed a peak oxidation current of 75 mA/mg and 365 mA/mg for DME and MF, respectively, highest among the studied Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72 and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT. The onset potential of DME on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst shifted negatively by ∼200 mV compared to PtRu. Irrespective of the support material, the ternary catalysts showed a significant negative shift in the onset and peak potential of COad oxidation compared with commercial PtRu. The oxidation behavior of MF was studied in conjugation with its hydrolysis products methanol, formic acid, and mixed fuel (formic acid + methanol) solution, which disapproves the earlier proposed acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of MF to a significant degree during the electrochemical experiment. Hence, the CNT-NCDs supported ternary catalyst showed high catalytic activity and stability for electro-oxidation of DME and MF.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, methyl formate (MF) and dimethyl ether (DME) electro-oxidation was studied on equimolar ratio PtPdSn catalyst supported on Vulcan Carbon XC-72, carbon nanotubes (CNT), and CNT–nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) composite. The ternary catalyst was synthesized by the ethylene glycol-assisted thermal reduction method and NCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method in the presence of CNT to form CNT-NCDs composite, in which the NCDs are incorporated onto the CNT surface. The activity of the catalyst in the oxidation of MF and DME was analyzed using cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry techniques. The ternary catalyst supported on CNT-NCDs composite (Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs) showed a peak oxidation current of 75 mA/mg and 365 mA/mg for DME and MF, respectively, highest among the studied Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72 and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT. The onset potential of DME on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst shifted negatively by ∼200 mV compared to PtRu. Irrespective of the support material, the ternary catalysts showed a significant negative shift in the onset and peak potential of COad oxidation compared with commercial PtRu. The oxidation behavior of MF was studied in conjugation with its hydrolysis products methanol, formic acid, and mixed fuel (formic acid + methanol) solution, which disapproves the earlier proposed acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of MF to a significant degree during the electrochemical experiment. Hence, the CNT-NCDs supported ternary catalyst showed high catalytic activity and stability for electro-oxidation of DME and MF.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct Liquid Fuel Cells (DLFCs) have received significant attention in recent years as potential power sources for mobile and portable devices [1,2]. DLFCs are divided into many subcategories, based on the utilized fuel, such as direct methanol fuel cell [3,4], direct ethanol fuel cell [5], and direct formic acid fuel cell [6,7]. These liquid fuels have few advantages over H2, such as high volumetric energy density, and ease of fuel storage and transportation [1]. However, the oxidation of these fuels is kinetically sluggish (methanol, ethanol), thus requiring expensive platinum group metal (PGM) or Pt alloy-based catalysts [8]. The crossover of fuel from the anode to the cathode side through the hydrated Nafion® membrane also deteriorates the fuel-cells performance due to a mixed potential effect [9]. Moreover, these fuels are supplied as diluted aqueous solutions which may limit their diffusion to the electrode and the removal of gaseous oxidation product [10]. Dimethyl ether (DME) and methyl formate (MF) have drawn attention in recent years due to their high energy density and low crossover through Nafion® membranes [11,12]. The physical properties of different fuels are compared in Table S1. These low boiling point fuels may be supplied to the fuel-cell at high concentrations in their gaseous form, without active pumping. However, the kinetics of electro-oxidation of these fuels are also sluggish on commercial catalysts, which hinder their commercialization. Optimization of the catalyst for oxidation of these small organic molecules requires a better understanding of the electro-oxidation mechanism on Pt-based surfaces.

Methanol anodic reaction starts with successive dehydrogenation steps, followed by the formation of strongly adsorbed COad, which readily poison PGM catalysts [13]. Hence, the direct methanol fuel cell requires a COad tolerant anode catalyst, which has a high rate of COad removal [14]. The formic acid (FA) undergoes oxidation through a dual pathway mechanism (direct and indirect pathways). The direct pathway bypasses the formation of COad, whereas the indirect pathway involves a rate-limiting step of COad removal [Eqs. (2) and (3)]. Since Pd-based catalyst, which promotes FA oxidation through the direct pathway, is not completely COad tolerant, the direct formic acid fuel cell catalysts also need the additional electronic capabilities to induce COad stripping at the anode [15,16]. MF undergoes acid-catalyzed hydrolysis [Eq. (4)] to form methanol and FA which are oxidized through their respective pathways [Eqs. (1)–(3)]. MF oxidation mechanism has not been extensively studied, but COad has been identified as the main poisoning intermediate [17]. DME oxidation on Pt surface starts with successive dehydrogenation steps followed by breaking the COC backbone, which results in the formation of COad [18–20]. It is clear from the above discussion that the COad is the main poisoning intermediate in the oxidation of the aforementioned small organic molecules. Dehydrogenation of one molecule of water is required for oxidative removal of COad via the formation of OHad [Eq. (6)] [21]. The OHad formation on the Pt/Pd surface begins at 0.70 V and takes place at a considerably higher rate above 0.90 V. To reduce the over-potential of OHad formation, Pt has been alloyed with oxophilic metals (Ru, Sn, W, Mo, and Sb) [22]. Various bimetallic materials have been proposed. Among these PtRu is considered as the state-of-the-art catalyst for the oxidation of methanol but not necessarily for MF and DME.
(1) CH3OH + H2O → CO2 + 6H+ + 6e−
(2) HCOOH + Me → CO2 + 2H+ + Me + 2e− (Direct Pathway)
(3) HCOOH + Me → Me–CO + H2O (Indirect Pathway)
(4) HCOOCH3 + H2O → HCOOH + CH3OH
(5) CH3OCH3 + 3H2O → CO2 + 12 H+ + 12 e−
(6) Me–COad + Me–OHad → 2Me + CO2 + H+ + e−

The inefficiency of PtRu toward oxidation of small organic molecules (except methanol) dictates that the catalysts specifically designed for one fuel can't be interchangeably used to operate with other fuels in DLFC. Besides PtRu, PtSn alloys have been extensively examined in the anodic reaction of methanol and ethanol. It was observed that COad electro-oxidation on Pt electrodes having a monolayer of Sn ad-atoms shifts the peak by more than 0.40 V in the lower overpotential [23]. Shubina showed that in contrast to Ru, CO binds only to Pt and not to Sn atoms, whereas OH has an energetic preference for the Sn sites, which implies that PtSn is a good COad oxidation catalyst [24]. Recently, we reported high activity of Sn@Pt–Sn catalyst for methanol oxidation [4] and SnO2@PtxPd1-x for DME oxidation [25]. A significantly lower PtCO adsorption energy was calculated using a DFT model of thin Pt layers over multi-layer Sn bulk core material [26]. Other reports describe the higher activity of PtPdRu than PtRu in methanol, DME, and MF oxidation [27,28] and suggested that ternary catalysts could be utilized in DLFC operating in a broader selection of fuels.

The proper dispersion of the catalyst on the supporting material is crucial for better utilization of precious metal and improves the power output of the fuel cell [29,30]. Generally, carbon-based materials, such as graphite powder, Vulcan carbon (XC-72), black pearl carbon (BP-2000), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), have been extensively investigated as support materials [31,32]. Other support materials such as CeO2, SnO2, and SiO2 have also been examined but these materials suffer from low conductivity [25,33,34]. Among the carbon-based catalysts supports, CNT has been reported as a preferred material attributed to the high surface area, electronic conductivity, and chemical stability [35]. Carbon quantum dots has been employed in electro-catalysis for hydrogen evolution [36], oxygen reduction [37], and methanol oxidation reaction [38]. It has been observed that the catalytic activity of Pt-NCDs is superior to that of PtRu/C for methanol oxidation [38]. The high activity was ascribed to the abundant functional groups present on the surface of NCDs which facilitate nucleating and anchoring points to the formation of Pt catalysts with a small size and a uniform distribution. Moreover, the NCDs can provide a rapid route for the activation of water, which favors the accelerated oxidative removal of COad. In this work, we intended to develop an efficient fuel flexible catalyst, especially for DME and MF oxidation, by harnessing the benefits of high surface area CNT-NCDs composite support and equimolar PtPdSn ternary catalyst. The main motive behind selecting a ternary catalyst in which active metals were in an equimolar ratio was the superior activity for DME oxidation observed on Pt1Pd1@SnO2 in our earlier studies [25] and similar behavior was reported for MF oxidation on PtRuPd [27]. The ternary catalyst supported on CNT-NCDs composite (Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs) is expected to show enhanced catalytic performance for oxidation of both DME and MF due to the synergistic effect of Pt, Pd, and Sn.

Amino acid (99.99%), multi-walled CNT (99.999%), tin chloride (SnCl2, 99.95%), DME (CH3OCH3 ≥ 99%), lithium chloride (LiCl, 99.0%), and MF (HCO2CH3, 99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium tetracholoroplatinate (K2PtCl4, 99.9%) and palladium chloride (PdCl2, 99.9%) were purchased from STREM chemicals. Formic acid (HCOOH, 95%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95–97%), ethylene glycol (HOCH2CH2OH, 99.5%), and methanol (CH3OH, 99.9%) were purchased from Merck. Double deionized water (∼18.2 MΩ) was used throughout the experiments.

In brief, 0.30 g amino acid (tryptophan) was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 10 min at room temperature (25 °C) to prepare a homogeneous aqueous solution. The resulting solution, after adding the appropriate amount of CNT, was transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a hot-air oven. After completion, the reaction was quenched by cooling the autoclave in a water bath. The precipitate of CNT-NCDs nanocomposites was collected from the product solution via centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The synthesized product was washed with deionized water and ethanol (until the pH of the solution becomes neutral) and lyophilized. Subsequently, the dried powder was manually grounded into a fine powder with a corundum mortar.

The aqueous solution LiCl (2.0 mL, 0.01 M) was prepared in which an appropriate amount of PdCl2 was dissolved using bath sonication. LiCl was used to enhance the solubility of PdCl2. The final volume of solution was increased to 25 mL by adding ethylene glycol followed by the addition of an appropriate amount of K2PtCl4 was added and dissolved using bath sonication. The resultant solution was stirred for 1 h and finally, SnCl2 was added. An appropriate amount (30% by weight) of CNT-NCDs was dispersed in ethylene glycol by horn sonication and added to the solution. The resultant solution was slowly heated in an oil bath up to 100 °C, then the temperature was raised to 200 °C, and the solution was refluxed for 2 h. The resultant dark black precipitate was washed several times with water and acetone and finally dried under vacuum for 4 h.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed with a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation operating at 40 kV/30 mA with a 0.02 step size and one step sec−1. The morphology of CNT-NCDs nanocomposites was analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using a JEOL 2100 (JEOL USA, Inc., Peabody, MA 01960, USA) microscope operated at 200 kV. The samples for the HRTEM analysis were prepared by adding some powder of CNT-NCDs to 5 mL of isopropanol and bath sonication for 2 min. The resultant mixture was dropped on a carbon-coated copper grid and then dried under vacuum at 25 °C for 12 h. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was performed with the Thermo Fisher Scientific, ESCALAB250 XPS system, (Theta Probe XPS system) using a monochromated Al K-alpha source at 15 kV and 150 W. The elemental composition of the catalyst was quantified by inductive coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (Amtec Spectro Arcos ICP-OES). The H/N/C/O elemental analysis was performed in Thermo Scientific™ Flash Smart™ Elemental Analyzer.

The electrochemical measurements were performed in a conventional three-electrode cell at room temperature using Bio-logic VSP electrochemical workstations. The catalyst ink was prepared by dispersing 5 mg of Pt1Pd1Sn1 and 20 μL of Nafion (5% in ethanol) in a 1 mL solution of water and isopropanol (1:1). The working electrode was prepared by drop-casting 20 μL catalyst ink on the Toray carbon sheet (geometrical area = 1 cm2). The potential was measured against the Ag/AgCl (3.0 M) reference electrode and converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale. A platinum mesh was used as a counter electrode. Electrochemical testing was performed in 0.5 M H2SO4 (supporting electrolyte). The electrolyte was purged with N2 for 30 min to remove dissolved O2, and a blanket of N2 was maintained during the measurements. Both PtRu/C and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts were activated by repeatedly cycling in the potential range of 0.00–1.20 V, at the scan rate of 100 mV/s until a stable voltammogram was obtained. —COad stripping measurements were done by first purging the electrolyte with CO for 15 min, followed by applying a constant potential of 10 mV vs. RHE. Then the solution was purged with nitrogen for 20 min to remove residual CO. few consecutive CV cycled were measured at a scan rate of 10 mV/s.

The crystallinity of the CNT, CNT-NCDs, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs was analyzed by XRD and presented in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peak of CNT shows a peak at 2θ = 26.2°, which is ascribed to the C(002) reflection of a hexagonal graphite structure. The other characteristic diffraction peak of graphite at 2θ = 45.0° is ascribed to the C(100) plane [39]. The diffraction pattern of CNT-NCDs demonstrates a peak at 2θ = 44.90°, which corresponds to the graphitic nature of both CNT and NCDs, but the peak became slightly broader, which represents poor crystallinity of graphitic carbon in NCDs. Similarly, the slightly broadening of the peak at 2θ = 25.5° also indicates poor crystallinity of the graphite structure in the CNT-NCDs [46]. The well-defined (002) peak shows the random orientation of CNT in our samples. In the case of CNT with a tube axis perpendicular to the substrate surface, the incident x-ray beam is scattered inside the sample and no signal is collected [39,40].

The diffraction pattern of the Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs exhibits peaks at 39.80°, 46.30°, 67.7°, and 81.5°, which corresponds to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes, respectively. These characteristic peaks correspond to the face-centered cubic (FCC) phase (PtPdSn reference pattern, PDF 00-029-0968). Besides the metallic phase of the ternary catalyst, no peaks of any metal (Pt, Pd, Sn) oxide were detected. The shift in Pt1Pd1Sn1(111) peak to lower angle with respect to Pt(111) shows lattice expansion due to the incorporation of Pd and Sn into the lattice of Pt. The Pt1Pd1Sn1(111) peak negatively shifted by 0.23° compared to Pt(111). The lattice parameter of Pt1Pd1Sn1 was 0.390 nm compared to 0.385 nm of Pt, calculated using the (111) peak. It has been reported that the PGM forms nearly all possible alloys with Sn and a shift of the peaks to lower angles should reveal the formation of a solid solution due to the incorporation of Sn in the FCC structure of PGM [41]. The crystallite size of the catalyst calculated from Scherer's equation was 5–6 nm.

The HRTEM images of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs are presented in Fig. 2 (a–d). HRTEM micrographs were obtained with magnifications in the range of 150,000–800,000. It was observed that the pristine CNT is aligned in the form of bundles with an average diameter of 30 nm and a length of a few micrometers (Fig. 2a). The NCDs have a spherical shape with an average diameter of 6.5 nm, determined by a statistical analysis of more than 200 particles (Fig. S2), and are embedded on the bundles of CNT (Fig. 2b). It was also observed that the NCDs decorated on the surface of CNT bundles with negligible aggregation, forming CNT-NCDs nano-composites. No un-bonded NCDs nanoparticles were observed around the CNTs. The HRTEM image showed that the catalyst nanoparticles are agglomerated into larger particles having a size in the range of ∼20–25 nm (Fig. 2c–d). Since a particle consists of several crystals, the observed particle size in HRTEM was higher than the crystallite size calculated from the XRD. Fig. 2(e) shows the electron diffraction pattern of Pt1Pd1Sn1 catalyst, which also confirms the FCC structure. The lattice parameters (0.391 nm) and crystallite size (5–6 nm) of the ternary catalyst calculated from electron diffraction were in the same range as was calculated from the XRD measurements. Fig. 2(f) shows the localized energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis of the catalyst, which confirms that the element's atomic ratio in the catalyst was the same as in the precursors (Pt: Pd: Sn = 1: 1: 1). The elemental ratio was further confirmed by the ICP-OES analysis.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the wide spectra of pure CNT in which only C1s peak is observed. The C1s peak at 284.8 eV was used as an internal standard for the determination of the binding energies of other elements [42]. Deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s peak (Fig. 3b) shows the presence of CC (sp3, 285.6 eV) and CC (sp2, 284.7 eV) bonds. Fig. 3(c) displays the oxygen spectra of pristine CNT in which presence of CO (sp3, 533 eV) bond confirms the presence of OH group. Fig. 3(d) shows the wide XPS spectrum of CNT/NCDs in which C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks are clearly observed at 284.58 eV, 400.75 eV, and 531.34 eV, respectively, whereas the N1s peak is not observed for pristine CNT. Based on C/H/N/S/O chromatogram analysis, the total N content in NCDs and CNT-NCDs was 17% and 1.5%, respectively. Fig. 3(e) shows the deconvoluted peaks of C1s, which exhibits a proof for the presence of CC (sp3, 285.6 eV), CC (sp2, 284.7 eV), CN/CO (sp3, 286.5 eV), and NHCO (sp2, 288.5 eV) bonds [43]. The XPS analysis clearly indicates the presence of oxygen-containing groups, such as COOH and OH in the pristine NCDs (Fig. 3f).

Fig. 4 (a) shows a wide XPS spectrum of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst, in which the C1s, Pt4f, Pd3d, Sn3d, and N1s peaks were observed. The deconvoluted C1s peak of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs (Fig. 4b) also showed presence of CC (sp3, 285.6 eV), CC (sp2, 284.7 eV), CN/CO (sp3, 286.5 eV), and NHCO (sp2, 288.5 eV) bonds. Moreover, the presence of oxygen-containing groups, such as COOH and OH bonds, present in the NCDs were also observed. Fig. 4(f) shows the deconvoluted N1s spectra. The first two binding energies (399.8 and 400.4 eV) are related to pyrrolic and pyridinic nitrogen atoms bound to carbon. The peak at 401.5 eV can be assigned to nitrogen atoms bound to graphitic carbon atoms [44]. A Pt4f peak is observed at 69.8 eV along with a shakeup peak at 73.1 eV, which corresponds to Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2, respectively (Fig. 4c). The deconvolution of the Pt 4f spectra revealed that it is mostly present in a metallic state Pt0 (82%), with a minor portion of Pt2+ (18%) [45]. The Sn3d signal also splits into two peaks centred at 495 eV and 487 eV, which corresponds to Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2, respectively (Fig. 4d). The first peak observed at 486.6 was ascribed to the Sn0, and the second peak at (495.0 eV) can be assigned to the Sn2+ and Sn4+ species [45]. The total percentage of Sn0 and Sn2+/Sn4+ in the catalyst was 75% and 25%, respectively. It is difficult to distinguish Sn2+ and Sn4+ oxidation states by XPS due to minor differences in the peak position. The peaks at 343.0 eV and 337.1 eV correspond to Pd2+, and the peaks at 341.0 eV and 335.2 eV correspond to Pd0 (Fig. 4e). The total percentage of Pd0 and Pd2+ in the catalyst was 64% and 24%, respectively. Owing to the electron-withdrawing effect of tin, both Pd0 and Pt0 peaks showed a positive shift (+0.45 eV) [41]. For enhanced catalytic activity and stability, the elements should be found in a metallic state. The XPS spectra represent the oxidation states of surface atoms in the sample. The surface atoms of the catalyst might have formed surface oxides which are observed by XPS. However, the XRD results showed the absence of any metal oxides, which means that the elements in the bulk material exist in their metallic state.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the voltammograms of Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts recorded in N2 saturated electrolyte. All catalysts showed a typical HUPD region in the potential range of 0.0–0.35 V (vs. RHE) and the capacitive region between 0.40 and 0.60 V. The formation of oxides on the catalyst surface starts at 0.80 V in the anodic sweep, whereas they are reduced back during the cathodic sweep at a potential range of 1.00 V–0.60 V. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the catalysts was calculated from the integration of the hydrogen atoms adsorption-desorption charge using Eq. (7) [46].
(7) ECSA = QH/([Pt] × 0.21)
where [Pt] represents the platinum loading (mg/cm2) on the electrode, QH is the integrated charge of the hydrogen desorption (mC/cm2) and 0.21 represents the charge required to oxidize a monolayer of H2 adsorbed on Pt. The calculated ECSA of Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts were 23.2 m2/g, 34.3 m2/g, and 35.3 m2/g, respectively. The ECSA of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts was higher than the Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, which is attributed to the high surface area of CNT and CNT-NCDs substrate compared with carbon XC-72. The ternary catalyst shows high stability under potential cycling (Fig. S1a). After 5000 cycles, the decrease in ECSA was less than 10% of the initial value.

Fig. 5(b) shows the voltammograms of MF oxidation on these catalysts at the anodic potential region. The peak oxidation current was normalized to the total content of PGMs in the catalyst. The onset potential of MF oxidation on Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs was 0.25 V. All three catalysts showed oxidation peak centered at 0.90 V, along with a shoulder peak centered at 0.70 V. The observed peak oxidation currents on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs at 0.70 V and 0.90 V were 144 mA/mg and 365 mA/mg, respectively. The current density observed at 0.70 V on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs was approximately two-fold higher than Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72 and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT. The total oxidation charge on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs was also higher than other ternary catalysts. The MF oxidation charge values on Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs were 670 mC, 760 mC, and 1160 mC, respectively. The peak oxidation current and the total charge are compared in Table S2. The voltammetry results confirm that the ternary catalyst supported on CNT-NCDs showed higher activity in terms of peak current and total oxidation charge in low (<0.70 V) and high potential (>0.70 V) regions. The high activity of the Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst is ascribed to the synergetic role of elements (Pt, Pd, Sn), the high surface area of CNT-NCDs composite substrate, and the bifunctional effect provided by the functional groups present in NCDs.

Above 0.90 V, the high coverage of surface oxide on the catalyst inhibits the adsorption of fuel, which results in a decline in the oxidation current on both catalysts [13]. Two oxidation pathways were proposed for MF oxidation in earlier reports [12,27]. According to the first pathway, MF undergoes acid-catalyzed hydrolysis in the acidic electrolyte to form FA and methanol, which then undergo oxidation through their respective pathways. The shoulder peak on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs represents the oxidation of FA through the direct pathway (Eq. (2)) and the main peak at 0.90 V represents methanol and FA oxidation through the indirect pathway [Eqs. (1) and (3)]. Although a dual pathway mechanism has also been proposed for methanol oxidation, it is mainly oxidized through the indirect pathway [Eq. (1)] [13,47]. In the low potential region (<0.70 V), FA is preferentially adsorbed and oxidized due to low activation overpotential compared to methanol. As the potential increases, FA depletes in the vicinity of the electrode due to fast oxidation kinetics, which allows methanol to get adsorbed and be oxidized in the high potential region (>0.70 V) [27].

According to the second pathway, MF is not hydrolyzed to a significant degree on the time scale of the electrochemical measurements, and thus, the formate part of the un-hydrolyzed MF is oxidized at the low potential region, which leads to the formation of methanol [Eq. (8)]. At the high potential region, methanol is oxidized as per its respective mechanism [48]. The shoulder peak of MF oxidation on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs represents FA/Formate oxidation through the direct pathway [Eq. (2)]. The peak current at 0.90 V represents COad oxidation, which is formed during methanol and FA oxidation through the indirect pathway [27,48]. Since Pd does not have any promotional effect on COad oxidation, the enhancement in the peak current observed at higher potentials is attributed solely to the presence of the oxophilic element (Sn) in the catalyst. It was reported that COad oxidation to CO2 and water dehydrogenation is a competitive process on Ru, whereas on Sn atom only water dehydrogenation takes place [24]. Therefore, the Sn sites are always free for the formation of OHad which facilitates COad removal from adjacent PGM sites. We also reported earlier that Sn atoms readily form surface oxides which provide extra oxygen atoms required for COad oxidation at low potential (∼0.40 V) as compared to Ru (0.80 V) [49]. Moreover, the better dispersion of the catalyst on the high surface area CNT-NQDs enhances the anodic current of MF and its constituents (methanol and FA).
(8) CH3OCOH + H2O → CH3OH + CO2 + 2H+ + 2e–

To examine the mechanism of MF oxidation on the tested catalyst in the acidic electrolyte, the electro-oxidation behavior of MF was compared with FA, methanol, and mixed fuels (methanol + FA) solution. Fig. 5(c) shows the voltammograms of FA oxidation on the studied catalysts. The onset potential of FA on Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts was 0.20 V, which is 0.05 V lower than MF. Moreover, all three catalysts showed two oxidation peaks centered at 0.50 V and 0.90 V, which corresponds to the FA oxidation through the direct and indirect pathways [27,48]. The peak current of FA oxidation on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs at 0.50 V and 0.90 V was 94 mA/mg and 206 mA/mg, respectively. The peak current and total oxidation charge on different catalysts are compared in Table S2. Overall, the peak current and oxidation charge of FA oxidation on the ternary catalyst was lower than MF. Taking into account the ability of Pd to effectively catalyze the electro-oxidation of FA through the direct pathway, the ternary catalyst showed higher activity for MF oxidation. The onset potential of methanol oxidation on these ternary catalysts was 0.30 V. The peak oxidation current on Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs at 0.82 V was 40 mA/mg, 177 mA/mg, and 264 mA/mg, respectively. Among ternary catalysts, the Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs showed the best activity for methanol oxidation. The peak currents and oxidation charges are compared in Table S2.

The oxidation behavior of MF was compared with the mixed fuel solution (FA + methanol) to examine the hypothesis of acid-catalyzed hydrolysis. Fig. 6 (a) shows the voltammogram of mixed fuel oxidation on ternary catalysts. Although the onset potentials and peak potentials of the mixed fuel oxidation on the ternary catalysts were lower than MF. The peak oxidation current and the oxidation charge were also lower than MF on respective catalysts. The Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst showed two oxidation peaks centered at 0.40 V and 0.90 V, whereas Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72 and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT showed only one oxidation peak along with a shoulder peak centered at 0.60 V. The peak oxidation currents at 0.90 V on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst for MF and mixed fuel were 365 mA/mg and 252 mA/mg, respectively. If MF hydrolyzes to a greater degree, the oxidation currents of both MF and FA should have been in the same range. However, the low activity of the ternary catalyst for the mixed fuel and the high activity for MF show an underlying difference in the oxidation mechanism. The higher onset potential of methanol oxidation, compared to MF and FA, shows that it cannot be oxidized at a significant rate in the low potential region (<0.50 V), therefore most of the current in case of mixed fuel is coming from the oxidation of the formate part of the ester.

If the MF dissociates to a greater extent to produce FA and methanol, the oxidation current of both mixed fuel and MF should be almost in the same range both in low and high potential regions. In this case of complete/higher degree hydrolysis of MF, the oxidation current in the low potential region (<0.70 V) mostly originates from FA oxidation, which is preferentially oxidized through the direct pathway due to preferential adsorption and faster oxidation kinetics than methanol. The depletion of FA in the vicinity of the electrode surface allows methanol to get adsorbed and oxidized at the high potential region (>0.70 V). Hence, one would expect the Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs to exhibit high catalytic activity during the oxidation of FA. But such behavior was not observed and it shows relatively poor performance during electro-oxidation of the mixed fuel solution (Fig. 6a). The difference in peak current of this solution and MF shows that the MF, may, to some extent, undergo solution-phase acid-induced hydrolysis to methanol and FA, but mostly does not dissociate on the time scale of the measurement. The formate component of the ester molecule was found to be oxidized more rapidly to release methanol [Eq. (8)], which then is oxidized in the high potential region. In this process, the methanol part of the ester is released later in the reaction therefore it does not hinder the oxidation of FA/formate. However, in the case of the mixed fuels solution methanol and FA are both present in the solution. Although methanol is oxidized in the high potential region, it is absorbed in the low potential region, hindering the adsorption and oxidation of FA. Hence, the observed oxidation current in this case, especially in the low potential region is lower than MF. The overall MF oxidation is represented by Eq. (9).
(9) CH3OCOH + 2H2O → 2CO2 + 8H+ + 8e–

The stability of the ternary catalyst and its tolerance to the poisoning intermediates was examined by chronoamperometry measurements. The surface of the catalyst was cleaned by holding the electrode potential at 1.00 V for 20 s, followed by switching the potential of the working electrode to 0.60 V for 60 min. The characteristic I vs. t curves of different fuels on Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs are presented in Fig. 6(b–d). Similar to the voltammetry experiments, the Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs showed the highest current density followed by Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT and Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72. The Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst showed the highest oxidation current for MF, followed by methanol and FA. It is clear from the above discussion that Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs showed the best activity for MF oxidation, which is also higher than commercial PtRu catalyst. The peak oxidation current on Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs was two times higher than commercial PtRu/C (Fig. S1b). The Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs could be a good anode catalyst for DLFC operated on MF, FA, and methanol. The high activity of the ternary catalyst is attributed to the ability of Pd to effectively catalyze the electro-oxidation of FA, a possible reaction intermediate or the ester ‘active component’ [27]. The high activity of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs as compared to other ternary catalyst is attributed to the high surface area and bifunctional/electronic effect provided by functional groups present on NCDs. The difference in electrochemical surface area of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs (35.3 m2/g) and Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72 (23.2 m2/g) is attributed to the high surface area of CNT, which allows proper dispersion of ternary catalyst. Moreover, the presence of functional groups on NCDs allows the COad removal at lower overpotential and shows higher activity than Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT.

Fig. 7 (a–d) shows the COad stripping voltammetry on the ternary catalysts. Irrespective of the support material, all ternary catalysts showed a sharp oxidation peak in the vicinity of 0.30 V vs. RHE. The observed onset potential of COad oxidation was much lower (∼300 mV) than on commercial PtRu catalyst, which is ascribed to the presence of tin oxide (SnOx) phase on the surface of the catalyst [49]. Although the tin oxide phase was not detected in XRD measurements (Fig. 2), the XPS analysis shows that ∼25% of the tin on the surface is present in the oxide form. All three catalysts showed a small peak at 0.80 V, which is probably coming from –COad oxidation on a small percentage of the unalloyed platinum/palladium. The ECSA of Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts calculated from –COad stripping were 22.5 m2/g, 32.3 m2/g and 38.5 m2/g, respectively, which is in good agreement with the values calculated from the HUPD region (Section 3.4.1). The CO oxidation peak current density attained in these measurements was 9.63 mA/mg, 10.87 mA/mg and 18.45 mA/mg for the same catalyst, respectively. Moreover, the Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs showed a negative shift of 10 mV in the peak potential. These results also emphasize the superior activity of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs due to its high ECSA and the functional groups present in NCDs which facilitates oxidative removal of COad at lower overpotential.

Fig. 8 (a) shows the voltammogram of DME oxidation on Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts. The concentration of DME saturated solution was 0.76 M at room temperature. As compared to cyclic voltammogram in N2 saturated electrolyte (Fig. 5a), suppression of HUPD region was observed in the presence of DME due to competition for absorption site. The observed onset potential of DME oxidation on all ternary catalysts was ∼0.30 V (vs. RHE), which is approximately the same as MF. A single oxidation peak is observed at 0.80 V. The peak oxidation current on Pt1Pd1Sn1/XC-72, Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT, and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts were 18 mA/mg, 30 mA/mg, and 75 mA/mg, respectively. The presence of oxygen-containing groups on NCDs facilitates COad removal at lower overpotential that is the main reason behind the high activity of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst. The DME oxidation on the best performing ternary catalyst was also compared with commercial PtRu/C.

Fig. 8(b) displays the comparison of DME oxidation on PtRu/C and Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst. The oxidation wave on commercial PtRu/C starts at 0.50 V and the peak current is observed at 0.80 V. The peak current observed in the anodic sweep was 60 mA/mg. The onset potential of DME oxidation on the ternary catalyst (ca. 0.30 V) is much lower than the value measured for PtRu (ca. 0.50 V). We reported earlier that the presence of alloyed tin significantly decreases the COad oxidation overpotential (∼0.30 V) [49]. The oxidation current decreases at potential above 0.90 V, i.e. in the region where the Pt surface undergoes more significant oxidation, it becomes less active for DME oxidation. The enhancement in activity is attributed to multiple factors; electronic effect provided Sn atoms in catalyst, the synergistic effect of palladium in breaking CO and CH bond [50], and ligand effect of tin atoms present on catalysts surface which facilitates COad oxidation by water dehydrogenation [23]. The high activity of Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs for DME oxidation was attributed to the functional groups present in NCDs which facilitates oxidative removal of COad by the bifunctional mechanism. The electrochemical results confirmed that Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalysts show higher activity for MF and DME oxidation than state-of-the-art PtRu/C. Moreover, the Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst also showed high activity for FA and methanol oxidation. The tin atoms in the catalyst facilitate COad oxidation by bifunctional effect, Pd atoms promote formate oxidation in MF and breaking COC bond in DME, and Pt atoms are the main adsorption sites for fuel. Moreover, the high surface area of CNT and CNT-NCDs composite compared to XC-72 carbon allows proper dispersion of the catalyst.

The ternary catalyst (Pt1Pd1Sn1) supported on Carbon XC-72, CNT, CNT-NCDs was synthesized by ethylene glycol assisted thermal reduction. The HRTEM images confirmed the formation of NCD doped CNT and the uniform distribution of the catalyst on the CNT-NCDs composite support material. The high surface area CNT facilitates the uniform distribution of the catalyst. The ICP and EDX analysis showed that the atomic composition in the attained catalyst was the same as in the starting materials. The FCC phase of the catalyst was confirmed by the XRD patterns. The Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs showed the highest electrochemical surface area of 35.3 m2/mg among all ternary catalysts. The electrochemical testing confirmed that the Pt1Pd1Sn1/CNT-NCDs catalyst showed the best catalytic activity for both MF and DME oxidation. The enhancement in activity is attributed to multiple factors; high surface area of CNT-NCDs substrate and electronic effect provided by the Sn atoms in COad oxidation. Moreover, the functional groups present on NCDs also facilitate COad oxidation at lower overpotential. The voltammetry results are not sufficient for elaborate discussion on the oxidation mechanism of both fuels. Additional in-situ studies are required to comment on the extent of the acid catalyzed hydrolysis of MF.
